title: The Restoration
subtitle: Perry's black ships, the Boshin War, and the fifteen-year-old Emperor Meiji on a real throne
bullets: Commodore Matthew Perry's four black ships sailed into Edo Bay in July 1853 demanding Japan open to trade — the shogunate, with no military capable of resisting steam-powered cannons, signed the Treaty of Kanagawa in 1854 and ended 214 years of sakoku.

The 1858 Harris Treaty imposed extraterritoriality, 5% fixed tariffs, and five additional open ports, then Britain, France, the Netherlands, and Russia signed near-identical Ansei Treaties within weeks — the unequal treaty regime that would humiliate Japan for forty years.

Three southwestern tozama domains — Satsuma in Kyushu, Choshu at the western end of Honshu, and Tosa on Shikoku — quietly modernized their militaries with French and British weapons in the 1860s and built the coalition that would overthrow the Tokugawa shogunate.

The Tosa ronin Sakamoto Ryoma personally arranged the secret Satsuma-Choshu Alliance of 1866; he was assassinated in Kyoto in December 1867 just before the Meiji Restoration, removing the most influential mediator at the worst possible moment.

The fifteenth and last shogun, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, formally returned political authority to Emperor Meiji in the November 1867 Taisei Hokan, betting that no other faction had the administrative experience to govern — the bet failed within weeks.

The Boshin War (January 1868–June 1869) finished the overthrow: imperial forces broke the bakufu army at Toba-Fushimi, took Edo without a fight in May 1868, and finally ran down the holdout naval Republic of Ezo at Hakodate after a six-month siege.

On April 1868 the new government issued the Charter Oath, a five-article document promising public assembly, abolition of class restrictions, and 'the seeking of knowledge throughout the world' — the founding text of the new state.

Edo was renamed Tokyo ('Eastern Capital') in September 1868 and the boy emperor moved into the former shogun's castle, ending Kyoto's thousand-year run as imperial capital and completing the symbolic transfer of sovereignty.

title: Dismantling Feudalism
subtitle: Haihan Chiken abolishes the domains, conscription dissolves the samurai monopoly, the Iwakura Mission tours twelve countries
bullets: On July 14, 1871, Haihan Chiken dissolved the 270 feudal domains and replaced them with prefectures under direct central administration — the daimyo were pensioned off in Tokyo and eight centuries of decentralized warrior rule ended in a single edict.

The 1873 Conscription Law required three years of military service from every able-bodied male over twenty regardless of class, dissolving the samurai monopoly on bearing arms — the act that, more than any other, ended the warrior caste as a social fact.

In 1876 the government converted hereditary samurai stipends into one-time bonds and the haitorei edict banned the wearing of swords — roughly two million people lost their inherited income overnight, and many would soon turn to political resistance.

The 1873 Land Tax Reform replaced the variable rice-tax with a fixed cash tax of 3% of land value, established private land titles for the first time in Japanese history, and pushed millions of marginal farmers off the land into the new urban factories.

From December 1871 to September 1873 the Iwakura Mission — half the senior Meiji government plus 50 students — toured the United States and twelve European countries, came back convinced that Bismarckian Germany was the model and that catch-up modernization was the only route to surviving the unequal treaties.

The 1873 Seikanron debate over invading Korea split the government: Saigo Takamori and Itagaki Taisuke wanted to fight, the Iwakura Mission returnees vetoed the plan, the hawks resigned — Korea would be invaded twenty-one years later, on the modernizers' schedule.

Maejima Hisoka established Japan's modern postal service in 1871; the first 18-mile Tokyo-Yokohama railway opened in 1872 with the emperor on the inaugural train; the New Currency Act of 1871 introduced the yen — the country was being wired together as fast as foreign engineers could build the infrastructure.

title: The Satsuma Rebellion
subtitle: Saigo Takamori's last samurai war and the conscript victory at Shiroyama
bullets: Saigo Takamori, the most popular man in Japan, resigned from the government in October 1873 after losing the Korea debate and went home to Kagoshima with 600 Satsuma officers and soldiers — he spent the next three years running the Shigakko private military academies for 20,000 disaffected samurai.

The Saga Rebellion of 1874, the Shinpuren Revolt and Akizuki and Hagi rebellions of 1876 were all small samurai uprisings that the conscript army crushed within weeks — the pattern was clear that localized samurai resistance was futile, but that the pattern would not stay localized if Saigo moved.

From January to September 1877 Saigo led roughly 30,000 Satsuma samurai in the largest of the samurai uprisings — they besieged the imperial garrison at Kumamoto Castle for fifty-four days but could not break the conscript defenders, then were progressively pushed south by 60,000 imperial troops.

On September 24, 1877, the last 400 Satsuma rebels were cornered on Shiroyama hill in Kagoshima where Saigo had grown up — Saigo, wounded in the hip by a bullet around six in the morning, committed seppuku facing east toward the Imperial Palace and was beheaded by his lieutenant Beppu Shinsuke.

Imperial dead and wounded numbered roughly 7,000 and Satsuma roughly 20,000 out of the original 30,000-strong force — the conscript army of peasants had defeated the most fanatical samurai force the country could field, ending seven centuries of warrior politics in a single morning.

General Yamagata Aritomo, the Choshu samurai who had built the conscript army that destroyed Saigo, washed his old Boshin War comrade's head personally and arranged its burial — the standard account says Yamagata wept.

Saigo was officially rehabilitated by imperial pardon in 1889 and his statues went up in Tokyo including the famous one in Ueno Park — the legend that he was a loyal subject deceived by his lieutenants defanged the rebellion politically and absorbed him into the official iconography of Meiji loyalty.

title: Industrial Revolution by Decree
subtitle: Pilot factories, the zaibatsu, the gold standard, and the developmentalist state model
bullets: The Tomioka Silk Mill opened June 1872 in Gunma Prefecture with French machinery and engineer Paul Brunat directing 400 young women from samurai families — the flagship pilot plant of the developmentalist state model that built dozens of private silk mills and made Japan the world's largest silk exporter by 1900.

The four great zaibatsu industrial conglomerates — Mitsui (descended from a Kyoto kimono merchant house), Mitsubishi (founded by Iwasaki Yataro the Tosa samurai turned shipping magnate), Sumitomo (the Besshi copper family), and Yasuda (the banking giant) — bought up state pilot factories cheap and dominated Japanese industry until 1945.

Finance minister Matsukata Masayoshi pursued a deliberate deflation in 1881–85 that crashed agricultural prices and pushed peasants off the land but stabilized the yen — he then founded the Bank of Japan on the Belgian central-bank model in 1882.

Using the 360-million-yen Chinese war indemnity from the Treaty of Shimonoseki, Japan adopted the gold standard in 1897, joining the international financial system on the same terms as the European powers — the Russo-Japanese War would soon be financed partly by foreign loans floated in London and New York.

The Yawata Steel Works opened in northern Kyushu in 1901 with German equipment from Krupp, was the first integrated steel mill in Asia, and produced 80% of Japan's steel by 1913 — the strategic asset that finally made the navy and the railways independent of imports.

By 1890 Japan had 1,400 miles of railway; by 1900, 3,600 miles; by 1910, over 5,000 miles — the 1906 Railway Nationalization Law consolidated the major private companies into state-owned Japanese Government Railways, the network that with successor reorganizations still operates as Japan Railways today.

Roughly 200,000 Japanese left for the Americas during the Meiji period, founding the diasporic communities that exist today — the Meiji industrial revolution was paid for in large part by the Japanese rural population, whose displaced sons and daughters supplied the factory labor and the emigration.

title: The Constitution and the Diet
subtitle: Ito Hirobumi's Prussian model, the 1889 Constitution, the 1890 Diet, and the genro who actually ran things
bullets: From 1874 the Freedom and People's Rights Movement led by Itagaki Taisuke demanded an elected national assembly — the government's response was the 1881 imperial promise that a constitution would be promulgated and a parliament convened by 1890.

Ito Hirobumi spent eighteen months in Berlin and Vienna in 1882–83 studying constitutional law under Lorenz von Stein and Rudolf von Gneist, returning with the Prussian model fully in his head — the constitution he drafted in 1889 would be unmistakably Bismarckian.

Ito Hirobumi became Japan's first prime minister on December 22, 1885 when the Cabinet System replaced the older Council of State, and the 1884 Peerage Act created a five-rank European-style nobility to staff the upper house of the future parliament.

The Meiji Constitution was promulgated February 11, 1889 on Kigensetsu — the legendary anniversary of Emperor Jimmu's founding of the imperial line — presented as a 'gift from the emperor' to the Japanese people rather than negotiated with any representative body.

The constitution gave the army and navy the right of supreme command (tosuiken), reporting directly to the emperor outside cabinet control — the structural separation that would prove catastrophic in the 1930s when the Kwantung Army could seize Manchuria without civilian authorization.

Japan's first Imperial Diet convened November 29, 1890 — the lower House of Representatives elected by male taxpayers paying at least 15 yen in direct taxes, about 1% of the population or 450,000 voters in a country of 40 million.

The genro elder statesmen — Ito, Yamagata, Inoue, Kuroda, Matsukata, Saigo Tsugumichi, Oyama — were the unofficial council that selected prime ministers and effectively governed Japan from outside the constitution they had written, with no legal standing and no successor body when the founders died in the 1910s and 1920s.

By 1899 extraterritoriality was abolished and by 1911 the last tariff restrictions were lifted — the thirty-year campaign to revise the unequal treaties that had been the original purpose of the Meiji project was finally complete on the eve of Emperor Meiji's death.

title: Education and Cultural Engineering
subtitle: Universal schooling, the Imperial Rescript on Education, kokutai, and the destruction of forty thousand Buddhist temples
bullets: The 1872 Education Order established universal compulsory primary education for both sexes in 53,760 elementary schools — by 1910 enrollment reached 95% and Japan had probably the highest literacy rate in the world, the human-capital base on which the entire Meiji industrial revolution depended.

Tokyo Imperial University, founded 1877, modeled on the German university system and funded as the elite training institution for the new bureaucratic civil service — by 1900 it was producing graduates competitive in international scientific research.

Mori Arinori, the Satsuma diplomat who designed the centralized Meiji school system as education minister 1885–89, was assassinated by a Shinto fanatic on the day the constitution was promulgated, accused of disrespecting the Ise Shrine — an early sign of how nationalist religion could turn on the modernizers.

The Imperial Rescript on Education issued October 30, 1890 over Emperor Meiji's signature made loyalty to the throne and Confucian filial piety the moral foundation of Japanese schooling, recited daily in every classroom for the next fifty-five years — the central liturgical text of the Meiji ideological project.

The 1868 separation of Shinto and Buddhism (shinbutsu bunri) triggered the Haibutsu Kishaku iconoclasm that destroyed roughly 40,000 Buddhist temples — out of perhaps 100,000 that had existed in the late Tokugawa period — in the first decade of Meiji rule, with centuries-old statuary burned, melted down, or sold to Western collectors.

Yasukuni Shrine was founded in Tokyo in 1869 to enshrine the spirits of those who died fighting for the emperor — beginning with the Boshin War dead and continuing through every subsequent war, it remains one of the most politically contentious religious sites in East Asia today.

Kokutai ('national essence') ideology took the older Tokugawa-era kokugaku argument that Japan was a uniquely sacred polity descended from Amaterasu and systematized it into a doctrine taught in primary schools — by the 1930s the 1925 Peace Preservation Law would criminalize advocacy of altering the kokutai.

Natsume Soseki, Japan's greatest modern novelist, studied in London 1900–02 and produced Kokoro, Botchan, and I Am a Cat — his work explored the psychological dislocation of Meiji modernization, the loneliness of being neither traditionally Japanese nor properly Western.

title: The Sino-Japanese and Russo-Japanese Wars
subtitle: Japan defeats China for Korea and Taiwan, then defeats Russia for Manchuria and recognition as a great power
bullets: The First Sino-Japanese War of August 1894 to April 1895 was fought over Korea — the Japanese army overran the peninsula, the Japanese navy destroyed the Chinese Beiyang Fleet at the Yalu River with no losses of its own, and the Treaty of Shimonoseki ceded Taiwan, the Pescadores, and the Liaodong Peninsula to Japan.

Six days after the Treaty of Shimonoseki, Russia, France, and Germany delivered the Triple Intervention forcing Japan to return Liaodong to China — Russia then leased Liaodong for itself in 1898 and built the naval base at Port Arthur, the immediate provocation that produced the Russo-Japanese War nine years later.

The Anglo-Japanese Alliance signed January 30, 1902 was the first equal alliance between a European and an Asian power in modern history — Britain's neutrality clause was the diplomatic insurance that made the Russo-Japanese War possible by keeping Russia's potential allies out.

The Russo-Japanese War began February 8, 1904 with a Japanese surprise torpedo attack on the Russian Pacific squadron at Port Arthur — two days before the formal declaration of war, a Japanese naval tradition Western observers considered duplicitous and Japanese strategists considered legitimate.

From August 1904 to January 1905 General Nogi Maresuke besieged the Russian fortress at Port Arthur taking it after 60,000 Japanese casualties — Nogi lost both his sons in the siege and would commit ritual suicide seven years later following Emperor Meiji to the grave.

The Battle of Mukden in February-March 1905 involved roughly 600,000 troops on both sides and was the largest land battle in world history to that date, producing 75,000 Japanese casualties and 90,000 Russian — only the world wars of the twentieth century would exceed it.

On May 27–28, 1905, Admiral Togo Heihachiro destroyed the Russian Baltic Fleet at Tsushima — sinking 21 of 38 Russian ships and losing only 3 torpedo boats, the most decisive naval engagement since Trafalgar a century earlier and the first modern Asian victory over a European fleet in fair pitched battle.

The Treaty of Portsmouth signed September 5, 1905 gave Japan Korea, southern Sakhalin, and the Russian Liaodong lease but no indemnity — the Hibiya Riots of September 5–7 forced martial law in Tokyo, demonstrating that Meiji nationalism had developed a popular dynamic the oligarchs no longer fully controlled.

title: Annexation, Death, and Legacy
subtitle: Korea annexed, the emperor dies, General Nogi commits seppuku, and the legacy that would carry Japan to 1945
bullets: The 1905 Eulsa Treaty made Korea a Japanese protectorate with Ito Hirobumi as the first Resident-General — Korean Emperor Gojong's secret 1907 Hague Peace Conference appeal failed, Ito forced him to abdicate, and the Korean Righteous Army guerrilla resistance of 1907–10 cost roughly 12,000 Korean lives.

On October 26, 1909, the Korean nationalist An Jung-geun shot and killed Ito Hirobumi at Harbin railway station in Manchuria — Ito was sixty-eight, his death removed the last senior voice in Tokyo for a softer Korea policy, and An Jung-geun remains a national hero in modern South Korea.

The Japan-Korea Treaty of August 22, 1910 formally annexed Korea, ending the 519-year Joseon dynasty — thirty-five years of colonial rule followed, including forced adoption of Japanese names, suppression of Korean language in education, and conscription of Korean women into the comfort women system.

Emperor Meiji died July 30, 1912 at age fifty-nine of complications from diabetes and uremia — the September 13 state funeral was the first imperial ceremony broadcast by international press to the entire world, treated as the death of one of the great monarchs of the age.

On the night of the imperial funeral, September 13, 1912, General Nogi Maresuke committed ritual junshi suicide at his Tokyo home by seppuku — his wife Shizuko cut her own throat with a dagger; Nogi had been waiting since 1877 to expiate the loss of a regimental banner at Kumamoto in the Satsuma Rebellion.

Nogi's revival of junshi — formally banned by the Tokugawa in 1663 and not performed publicly for two centuries — divided Japanese opinion, with Mori Ogai writing The Abe Family in response and Natsume Soseki writing Kokoro around a parallel suicide explicitly citing Nogi as the trigger.

The Meiji project accomplished the most successful crash modernization in world history — universal literacy, industrial output rivaling second-tier European economies, sovereignty restored, constitutional government, a colonial empire — and built the institutional substrate that the militarists of the 1930s would weaponize into the catastrophe of 1937–45.

Modern Japan still contains the Meiji synthesis encoded in its institutional and cultural DNA — the imperial line, Yasukuni Shrine, the bureaucratic state descended from the Meiji bureaucracy, the keiretsu corporations descended from the zaibatsu — both halves of the Meiji inheritance, the success and the disaster, are still alive and still matter.
